## [00:00 - 03:03]

Okay, here's part three of creating pre-launch content and we're going to talk about creating screencap video. Now in terms of screencap video, there are many, many types of screencap. You can do it as slides like I'm doing right now. It's basically you create a presentation in PowerPoint or in Keynote. You can do a mind map or a process map. I have done this a bunch where in fact I am pretty sure that I pioneered the whole idea of doing screen capture video with a mind map. In fact, my whole first time I rolled out product launch formula, that was one of the things that I did both in the product and the pre-launch and that was just like a blue people's mind back then. That was like eight years ago. No one did it before I did it. At least that I ever saw at least in this market. So I really pioneered that. Lots and lots of people have gone on to do that. So mind maps can be great. Process maps, very similar to mind maps. You can also screencap a blog or a social media site. Now this is great for social proof. Say like later in your pre-launch, maybe your third piece of pre-launch content or maybe even do a piece of content after your cart is open where you go. If you had a blog post and there's a lot of great comments on that blog post or if you have a social media site where people are leaving you great comments and testimonials, fire up your screencap and go to that site and then go through some of the comments. Go through some of the discussion. Go to your blog. Go through some of the discussion, some of the comments. And it's just really, really powerful way to show if people are saying great things about you or if you got a mention in some media, go shoot a video of that. It can be very, very powerful social proof if you're actually showing people what other people think about you around the web. Along the same lines, you could do it in email. If someone sends you this great testimonial via email, instead of just showing them that testimonial like showing some text on a screen, you can actually fire up your screen capture and navigate to your email program and open up the email and it just looks so much more real and so much more authentic. This is something I did way back in 2005 and it was super effective back then. It's still effective. So basically you're letting people, it's just big time social proof when you do something like that. You can do it for a membership site. Maybe it's your third piece of pre-launch content and if you're selling, say a membership site, you can take them for a virtual tour where you're opening up the membership site and then you're navigating around and showing what's going on and you're capturing the entire

## [03:03 - 06:08]

experience. So you're giving people an experience of going inside your product if you did something like that. It can be very, very powerful. Another one is software. I mean if you are selling software, you can give a tour of the software. You can show how the software works. You can show the results you get from the software. Very, very effective way to use screen capture. Okay. Now in terms of screen capture, how do you actually do this? What are the tools we use? There are lots and lots of tools and they keep on getting better and better and better. I've been using screen capture since, well for over a decade I think, since 2003. And it's just the tools get better all along. Now on the Mac, I use ScreenFlow. That's the name of the program. And it's not a terribly expensive program. I think it's about, I think it might be $100. And that's actually what I'm using right now to record this is ScreenFlow. And they keep on coming out with new versions. They have really great upgrade policies and it's gotten so much better than it was when it started out maybe, I don't know about four years ago or something like that. You can also get Camtasia. Camtasia is a really powerful program on Windows. You know I'm not as big a fan of it on Mac. The last time I looked and of course they're coming out with, constantly coming out with new updates as well. So ScreenFlow and Camtasia are very competitive with each other. And I don't know, I haven't looked recently. Maybe Camtasia got a lot better. Last time I looked, I actually bought both of them and I tested them out and I like ScreenFlow better. But again, it could be different by the time you are watching this. Then there's also QuickTime. And that is built right into your Mac. So you basically have a free screen capture tool right within QuickTime where you can just make a screen recording is what they call it. Not as powerful or as flexible as ScreenFlow or Camtasia but the price is right. It's a great place to test it out. And on the Mac, there are a bunch of other screen capture tools as well. But ScreenFlow and Camtasia are certainly the top of the heap the last time I looked. Now Windows, it's a lot more limited. That's the bad news. The good news is that Camtasia is a great program on Windows. Now I haven't used this in several years because I switched over the Mac several years ago. But when I used Windows, I used Camtasia because it's just an extraordinary program. And it is quite a bit more expensive over on the Windows the last time I looked. It was like $300 or something like that. So it's very expensive. But there is a 30-day free trial. So you can check out the 30-day free trial. It's completely unlimited. So you have 30 days. ## [06:08 - 09:10]

and create your product and release it and pay for it theoretically before you even needed to actually buy it. So it's just a great free trial. It's a great program. There's also a free one called Cam Studio. And this one, it's been bought and sold a few times. At one point it was free. Then it wasn't free. I just went and looked and it looks like right now it's free. But this thing's bounced around quite a bit. I've never used it. I've heard of other people using it. How it compares to Camtasia, I don't know. But I just put it on there because it might be something you might want to check out. Okay, now continuing on the tools front, that software will capture the screen for you and it will capture audio for you. But you have to worry about, you know, the thing is, is audio quality is very important with your video. And this goes for whether you are using a screen capture or full motion video. The audio quality is very important. Use an external mic. Don't use your computer's built-in mic as a general rule. The built-in mics and computers are keep on getting better, but I would still use an external mic because they're not expensive. Headset mics are cheap and they work great. That's what I'm using right now. The great thing about them is they aren't impacted by the recording environment. What I mean by that is you could be, well, I'll just give you an example. One time I was, I shot, you know, created a screen cap video and then at my, at my home and then I was in at my office and I decided I wanted to add some to it. I had my headset mic with me and even though it was a completely different recording environment, I was able to, to just, you know, fire up my screen cap, record some audio and then plug that right in and you couldn't tell the difference. If you use a desktop mic or, you know, anything else other than a headset mic, it's very susceptible to your recording environment. The room you're in, the placement of the mic is critical. You know, whether, you know, what, what the walls are made of, what kind of floor covering you have can all impact a, you know, a regular mic that's either on your desktop or on a stand and it can be, you know, just, it's very susceptible to the environment and you, I mean, so if you change your environment, then you can't just go and mix in, you know, the mix different audios from different environments. Like that one time I told you I recorded with a headset and I recorded, you know, one part in my office, part at my home and then I actually had to go, I had, I took a trip and I, I flew across the country to actually do a different country and I was in my hotel room and I realized that I needed to make some changes. They weren't big but just a few changes to that screen cap video and I brought my, my microphone, my headset microphone just in case.

## [09:10 - 12:10]

Put that on my head, I'm sitting there in a hotel room in a different country, recording audio and you couldn't tell the difference between the different audios just because the nature of a headset mic with the, where you're close miced, meaning the mic is very close to your mouth, not so impacted by your external environment, by the recording environment. So that's why I like them and I tend to use them. They're also, they tend to be very cheap and they, they just, they work great. So I like, I've got a few of them, I've used a few of them. I like, the one I'm using right now is by Plantronics. It was probably about $120 mic. I've used, I've got one by Sennheiser. I really like a lot. I just got a new one from Logitech. They, it was a little bit smaller. I wanted something smaller that it could, you know, it would fit in my bag when I traveled. So any of those brands, you know, if you, you can really get a decent headset for 30 bucks, you know, a decent headset mic for 30 bucks. I think the ones I've got, I've spent mostly between 70 and $120, $150. Get a USB headset. So it just plugs right into your computer. Don't get one like with those little jacks, those little mini jacks or the XLXLR inputs. Just get one with USB. It makes it a lot simpler for you. One of the things, and this is actually, this is the times when I use, don't use a headset. When I use like a mic on my desk is because I don't want to look geeky. Like if you're actually going to be on camera, because like in some programs like ScreenFlow, you can be on camera at the same time where you're recording the screen. I could see you up in the corner of the screen and that can be pretty cool. But I don't want to, I don't like the way I look with a headset on. You look sort of like a geek. So in, or if I'm doing like some webcam video where I'm doing a webcam interview or something, then I'll use a desktop mic because I don't want to get that. I don't want to have the headset on. I just think it looks a little bit geeky. Some people do it and they pull it off, but I just don't like the way they look on me. So if you are going to use a desktop mic, they are a little trickier to set up. They are definitely impacted by the recording environment, especially by the placement of the mic. And you can't move around. You have to stay in the same place. So that means generally you're seated. You don't want to swivel around. You don't want to turn your head. You want to be talking directly into that mic. So that's very important. Now a lot of times when I'm recording a screen cap video, I want to be standing up because I tend to have more energy when I'm standing up. And often I'll even walk around a little bit with my headset mic on. And in fact right now I'm standing up. So I do think I bring more energy with a headset mic because I can move around.

## [12:10 - 13:16]

The other thing is if your chair might be noisy, it might rock, it might swivel, and when it does that it might creak. And when it does that and you're using a desktop mic, it will pick up those sounds. So just something to be aware of. Now the cool thing about desktop mics is you don't look geeky. In fact you can look Uber Pro if there's a shot of you and you've got a fancy mic that's partially in the shot. It can actually look sort of cool. Or at least some people can think it can look sort of cool. Again get a USB mic. Don't bother with the XLR in my opinion. If you get a USB mic you can plug it into any computer.
